title: Part 1: Lower binary search — Koko Eating Bananas and Split Array Largest Sum

cues.label: Lower template
cues.headline: lo < hi, mid = (lo+hi)/2
cues.narration: The lower binary search template finds the smallest feasible candidate by narrowing the range from both sides.

cues.label: Ceiling division
cues.headline: ceil(pile / speed) counts hours
cues.narration: Each pile takes ceiling of pile over speed hours. Use integer arithmetic: (pile + speed - 1) // speed.

cues.label: Split Array
cues.headline: Same template, greedy partition
cues.narration: Split Array Largest Sum uses the same lower search: candidate is the max allowed sum, feasibility is a greedy left-to-right scan counting subarrays.

cues.label: Range bounds
cues.headline: lo = max element, hi = total sum
cues.narration: For partition problems, lo is the largest element (no subarray can be smaller), hi is the total sum (one subarray holds everything).

cues.label: Zero init trap
cues.headline: lo = 0 breaks the feasibility check
cues.narration: Setting lo to zero tests a meaningless candidate: zero speed means infinite time, and zero max sum means no element fits. Always use the tightest meaningful lower bound.

cues.label: Greedy is optimal
cues.headline: Extend each subarray as far as possible
cues.narration: The greedy scan uses the fewest subarrays for a given target, so if greedy needs more than k, nothing else can do it in k either.
